ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Badam Milk (Indian spiced almond-milk drink). Closest match is almond milk beverage (FOODON_03302050); actual product contains full-cream dairy milk, almond paste, saffron, cardamom, and sweetener.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers milk-based beverages broadly. No specific code exists for Badam Milk; this is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Badam Milk or Indian spiced almond-milk beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Badam Milk is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing around 230 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 13 g fat, and 21 g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN) / ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Badam Milk is not listed as a single entry in NIN tables. Values are adapted from NIN component data for whole cow milk (full-cream) and almonds, combined in typical recipe proportions (approximately 85% milk, 10% almond paste, 5% sugar/spices), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for whole milk (FDC ID 746782) and almonds (FDC ID 170567). Micronutrient values are consistent with this composite approach.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for full-cream milk (GI ~27) and sugar-sweetened milk beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Badam Milk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from analogous entries: full-cream milk (GI ~27, University of Sydney GI Database), sweetened flavored milk beverages (GI ~34–48), and the moderating effect of almond fat and protein on sugar digestion. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.25) / 100 = 9.6 g. Classified as low GI per University of Sydney thresholds (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (165 mg/100 g), magnesium (25 mg/100 g), and calcium (125 mg/100 g) with low sodium (40 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Badam Milk is generally suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its favorable potassium-to-sodium ratio and vasodilatory minerals; however, added sugar and saturated fat from full-cream milk should be monitored, and low-fat or reduced-sugar versions are preferable for regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Contains 7.5 g sugar per 100 g (predominantly from added sugar and lactose) despite a low estimated GI of 45
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Badam Milk in moderation; substituting added sugar with a low-calorie sweetener such as stevia and using low-fat milk can reduce the glycemic and caloric load, and portion size should be limited to one small glass (150–200 ml) per occasion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Almonds provide heart-healthy monounsaturated fat and Vitamin E; saturated fat is moderate at approximately 2.1 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Badam Milk can be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions given its almond-derived monounsaturated fats and antioxidant Vitamin E content; full-cream milk contributes saturated fat, so individuals with established cardiovascular disease should prefer a low-fat milk base and limit added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saffron and almonds have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties; high Vitamin E content (1.2 mg/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Badam Milk may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the almond-derived Vitamin E and saffron compounds have anti-inflammatory potential; refined sugar should be minimized to avoid pro-inflammatory effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Badam Milk is a traditional Indian milk-based beverage prepared by simmering full-cream cow's milk with a fine paste of blanched and peeled almonds (badam), flavored with aromatic green cardamom and premium saffron strands, and sweetened with sugar, honey, or jaggery. The drink has a rich, creamy texture with a subtly nutty flavor, a warm golden color imparted by saffron, and a fragrant floral aroma from cardamom. It can be served warm in winter or chilled in summer, making it a year-round beverage. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 92 kcal, 3.8 g protein, 5.2 g fat (including heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from almonds), and 8.5 g carbohydrates, along with meaningful amounts of calcium (125 mg), potassium (165 mg), Vitamin E (1.2 mg), and riboflavin (0.18 mg). The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low), moderated by the fat and protein content of almonds and milk. Clinically, it is suitable for most healthy adults and children; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit added sugar and portion size; those with hypertension benefit from its potassium and magnesium content. It is deeply embedded in Indian culture as a festive welcome drink served at weddings and religious celebrations, and is traditionally given to children for its perceived cognitive and strength-building benefits. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal, but contains dairy and tree nut allergens.
common_user_queries: Is Badam Milk good for diabetes?
Can I drink Badam Milk for weight loss?
Is Badam Milk suitable for hypertension?
What are the benefits of Badam Milk?

confidence_reason: Badam Milk is not listed as a single composite entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN (ICMR) component data for full-cream milk and almonds combined in typical recipe proportions, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for full-cream milk and sweetened milk beverages. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability (almond-to-milk ratio, sweetener type and quantity) and the absence of a direct laboratory-analyzed entry for this composite beverage.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN/ICMR component data for full-cream cow milk and almonds in approximate recipe proportions (85% milk, 10% almond paste, 5% sugar/spices), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole milk FDC ID 746782; almonds FDC ID 170567). No single approved-source entry for Badam Milk as a composite dish was found. 2. Saturated fat (2.1 g), monounsaturated fat (1.8 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.7 g), trans_fat (0.0 g), cholesterol (12.0 mg), and water (82.0 g) are estimated from component proportions; detailed fatty acid profiles for this composite beverage are not independently reported in NIN or USDA as a single entry. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Badam Milk was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 4. The input record contained four duplicate portion entries (all '1 serving 250 ml'); deduplicated to a single default serving of 250 g/ml. 5. Input food_group was 'Snacks'; reclassified to 'Beverages' as Badam Milk is a liquid milk-based drink. 6. Almond Board of California (almonds.com) was listed as a source in the input; this is not an approved Veritas source and has been replaced with NIN/ICMR and USDA FoodData Central. 7. No -1 sentinel values remain undocumented; all -1 fields in the template have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component analysis except where noted above.
